question.text: What is the fundamental right that an option buyer purchases?

answer.text: The right, but not the obligation, to buy (call) or sell (put) the underlying asset at a predetermined price (strike) on or before a specified date
The obligation to buy or sell the underlying asset at the current market price
The right to receive dividends from the underlying asset without owning it
The right to extend the maturity of a futures contract

explanation.text: **The Logic:** An option gives the buyer asymmetric rights — they can choose to exercise or not. The buyer pays a premium for this right. The seller (writer) receives the premium but has an OBLIGATION if the buyer exercises. **The Trap:** Futures create obligations for both parties. Options are asymmetric — only the buyer has a choice. The right is to buy/sell at the STRIKE price, not the market price. **Cross-Exam Context:** This definition applies to all options — equity (VIII Ch4), currency (I Ch4), IR (IV Ch4), and commodity (XVI Ch4). Universal across Series XIII. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The asymmetric payoff is why options are preferred for hedging — you're protected against adverse moves but can benefit from favorable moves (minus premium paid).
